Relowa POC · v0.1
Home / Concepts / auth.uid() Pattern

auth.uid() Pattern

TypeConcept
💡

How we replicate Supabase's auth.uid() developer experience without Supabase, in 30 lines of SQL plus 5 lines of middleware.

Why this concept matters

The seamless flow every Supabase developer knows:

  1. User logs in → a JWT token is issued.
  2. App calls db.from('tenders').select() — no WHERE clause.
  3. Only their org's data comes back.

No application-side filtering. No middleware that checks org ownership. The "magic" that makes this work is vanilla Postgres, wired up once in the schema and never touched again.

The mechanism (3 pieces)

1. The GUC

Before every query, the middleware injects the JWT claims into a Postgres session variable:

-- Called by middleware at the start of every transaction
SELECT set_config('request.jwt.claims', '{"sub":"550e8400-...","email":"ahmet@acme.example","active_org_id":"7c9e6679-..."}', true);

The third argument true means local to this transaction. No other connection sees it. When the transaction commits or rolls back, the setting evaporates.

2. Helper functions

With the GUC set, SQL functions can read the claims and extract whatever the RLS policy needs:

-- Extract the authenticated user's UUID from the JWT
CREATE FUNCTION auth.uid()
RETURNS uuid
LANGUAGE sql
STABLE
SECURITY DEFINER
SET search_path = 'public', 'pg_temp'
AS $$
  SELECT COALESCE(
    nullif(current_setting('request.jwt.claims', true), '')::jsonb ->> 'sub',
    '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'
  )::uuid;
$$;

-- Extract the active org from the JWT
CREATE FUNCTION auth.org_id()
RETURNS uuid
LANGUAGE sql
STABLE
SECURITY DEFINER
SET search_path = 'public', 'pg_temp'
AS $$
  SELECT COALESCE(
    nullif(current_setting('request.jwt.claims', true), '')::jsonb ->> 'active_org_id',
    '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'
  )::uuid;
$$;

Now RLS policies can say things like USING (org_id = auth.org_id()) — the function is a single word, and its name communicates intent perfectly.

3. The middleware

The Hono middleware that extracts the JWT, verifies it, and calls set_config before every request handler runs:

import { createMiddleware } from 'hono/factory';
import { verify } from '@relowa/auth';

export const jwtGuc = createMiddleware(async (c, next) => {
  const token = c.req.header('Authorization')?.replace('Bearer ', '');
  const claims = token ? await verify(token) : null;

  await db.execute(sql`
    SELECT set_config(
      'request.jwt.claims',
      ${claims ? JSON.stringify(claims) : '{}'},
      true
    )
  `);

  c.set('claims', claims);
  await next();
});

That's it. Every downstream handler and every Postgres query now implicitly knows who the caller is and which org they represent — without a single WHERE clause in application code.

⚠️
Why SECURITY DEFINER matters

When auth.has_role('admin') queries the org_members table, it can trigger the RLS policy on org_members — which calls auth.has_role() again — which queries org_members — infinite recursion. The database hangs or errors.

The fix: mark every auth.* helper as SECURITY DEFINER. This makes the function run with the privileges of its owner (typically the postgres superuser or a bootstrap role), bypassing RLS on the tables it reads. The explicit SET search_path = public, pg_temp prevents search-path injection attacks.

-- The pattern every auth helper must follow
CREATE FUNCTION auth.has_role(role_name text)
RETURNS boolean
LANGUAGE sql
STABLE
SECURITY DEFINER            -- <--- THIS avoids RLS recursion
SET search_path = 'public', 'pg_temp'  -- <--- THIS prevents injection
AS $$
  SELECT EXISTS (
    SELECT 1 FROM org_members
    WHERE org_id = auth.org_id()
    AND user_id = auth.uid()
    AND role = role_name
  );
$$;

Anti-patterns

🚫
  • Using auth.uid() in application code. The function only works inside Postgres. Calling it from TypeScript returns nothing useful. Use c.get('claims').sub instead.
  • Using auth.uid() as a column default. Column defaults execute before the middleware sets the GUC. The value will always be the zero UUID.
  • Setting request.jwt.claims outside the middleware. If any other code path touches this GUC, the guarantee that "this transaction is authorized" vanishes. One call site. One.
  • Granting the application role superuser. If the app role can bypass RLS with SET bypassrls = on, the entire security model collapses. The app role should have zero superuser attributes.
See also
concepts/multi-tenancy adr/0003-rls-with-jwt-guc-pattern
